mistaken_claim: Creating more cards automatically leads to better learning.
why_it_seems_plausible: More cards can look like more accumulation, so it is easy to confuse quantity with understanding.
why_it_is_wrong: Card count does not equal conceptual depth.
Too many low-value cards dilute graph quality and increase maintenance cost.
correct_understanding: What matters more is card boundary clarity, content density, and whether a card is worth maturing.
what_it_confuses: It confuses 'recording more things' with 'understanding more deeply'.
representative_counterexamples: Splitting one topic into four shallow cards can produce a noisier graph instead of a better one.
trigger_signals: The user wants to create all four card types by default for one topic.
The discussion focus is already drifting, but card count keeps expanding.
corrective_action: Choose the strongest single card type first and create only one card.

common_confusions: [[Learning Card Routing]] | The misconception often appears together with wrong card-type selection.
correct_return_nodes: [[Learning Card Routing]] | After correction, routing should decide the right single-card path.
adjacent_misconceptions: [[More Structure Means Better Learning]] | Both confuse formal expansion with real understanding.

prerequisites: [[Single Concept Capture]] | Without a single-card boundary, this misconception keeps recurring.
enables: [[Learning Card Routing]] | Correcting this misconception makes later routing more stable.
contrasts: [[Single Concept Capture]] | One emphasizes conservative focus; the other represents expansion pressure.
corrections: [[Create All Four Cards by Default]] | This card explicitly corrects that behavior.

direct_routes: When a user wants four cards by default, correct the misconception first and then return to [[Learning Card Routing]].
If the real problem is not a misconception but unclear card boundaries, route directly to [[Single Concept Capture]].
secondary_routes: If [[Learning Card Routing]] still leaves ambiguity, continue to [[Single Concept Capture]] to re-establish a conservative one-card boundary.
gap_signals: If the graph keeps encountering quantity-over-quality mistakes but has no card for conservative card creation, add a new Misconception or Method card for that gap.
stop_rules: Stop treating the issue as a misconception once the user already accepts the correction and the problem becomes operational routing.

current_status_notes: This card already works as a stable local correction node and now has explicit return routes.
next_goal: Add stronger rules for which misconception to correct first when multiple learning-design misconceptions overlap.
growing_checklist: Explain why the misconception looks plausible.
stable_checklist: Provide enough node-level relationships to show where correction should return.
expert_ready_checklist: Add at least one explicit gap signal and one stop rule.

main_reasons: The card has stable corrective value and explicit return routing.
It still lacks stronger arbitration when multiple misconceptions overlap.
missing_evidence: It lacks a stronger priority rule for stacked misconceptions.
Its gap detection still needs more repeated evidence.
next_rules: If the user confuses quantity with understanding, correct this card before any routing work.
If the user already accepts the correction, stop here and resume operational routing.

current_upgrade_tasks: Capture one case where correcting this misconception still left a second misconception unresolved.
upgrade_history: 2026-03-27: migrated routing to the four-part dispatch structure
